evidence: Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.
The company that 'owns' a university's career pipeline owns 10 years of talent flow from that institution.

The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.
Career infrastructure, not career tool. Like a life operating system. Monetization happens through compounding relationship, not transaction.

source_display: The Black Pill on Talent Visibility
evidence: Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.
Has existing business relationship (sells annotation services to Handshake); has internal contacts. Actively searched LinkedIn for Handshake contacts during the call.

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters. This reframes product priorities, marketing messaging, and technical roadmap.

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: Ilse's acknowledgment that this is now the go-forward direction.

source_display: Response Rate as the Only Metric That Matters
evidence: Logan's insight: use response rates as the convincing proxy — 'our users pay attention to our recommendations'
It's not a security blanket — it's getting AI-skeptical recruiters to 'see that it does, in fact, generate some names worth paying attention to.'

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: The 'silent scan' becomes more than a feature—it's a philosophical stance about how hiring actually works vs. how it's supposed to work.

source_display: Information Efficiency Has Inverted Recruiter Value
evidence: I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.
Handshake discussion as 'perfect acquirer' with distribution to all colleges positions acquisition as strategic vs survival.
Superposition wouldn't bring users. They might not even bring significant revenue. But their logo creates a trust cascade. This is pure signaling economics—the brand IS the product.
V's counter to 'you only have 500 users' was essentially: 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' This inverts the network effects playbook.
Logan's counter: quality variance risk (e.g., '500 former teachers') - pushing for response rate over volume guarantees.
Pursue both brand-name partnerships (Superposition) for credibility AND hungry individual recruiters for execution.
AI-native recruiting firm. V believes SLA-based deal structure could work with Edmund. Brand names provide website legitimacy.
Potential partnership opportunity. V believes SLA-based deal structure could work with him. AI headhunter positioning.

source_display: The Sora Video as Product Vision
evidence: Ilse's recollection of a generated Sora video—an auditorium full of candidates that empties to three people after clicking a Careerspan button. The video captured what thousands of words of positioning couldn't: hiring is too loud, and we make it quiet.
